one_line_takeaway: MDLMs inherit AR models' vulnerability to data-poisoning backdoors, but their unique masked-denoising decoding lets a purely self-referential defense (DiSP) mask high-saliency visual tokens to rewrite poisoned training responses and fine-tune away the backdoor, cutting ASR from >90% to roughly 0-5% with no auxiliary model or clean data, and beating random-drop, pruning, and an oracle BYE-style filtering baseline across two MDLMs and three backdoor targets.
key_finding: Backdoored MDLMs (no defense) reach ASR (w/t) >= ~90% on triggered inputs (up to 98.50% for LLaDA-V targeted refusal) while ASR (w/o) stays under ~2.5% and clean performance drops by at most ~3% relative to the clean model -- confirming MDLMs are as backdoor-susceptible as AR models despite their different (masked-denoising) generation paradigm. DiSP reduces ASR (w/t) to 0.5-1.5% across nearly all LLaDA-V and LaViDa settings (e.g., LLaDA-V content insertion: 92.50% -> 0.50%, a -14.50 point-normalized/-92-point-absolute reduction described in the paper as '(-14.50)' relative to the CP baseline notation; LLaDA-V targeted refusal ASR w/t: 98.50% -> 1.00%; LaViDa content insertion: 93.50% -> 0.00%) while keeping CP within about 0.3 points of the backdoored model's CP in most cases. Baselines are markedly weaker on triggered ASR: on LLaDA-V, Random drop/Pruning/Data filtering leave ASR (w/t) at 91.50%/15.00%/74.50% respectively for content insertion, and DiSP beats all three baselines across every combination of model x attack target reported in Tables 1-2. A poison-ratio sweep (10/20/30/40/50%) on LLaDA-V content insertion shows baseline and no-defense ASR generally rising with poisoning rate while DiSP's ASR stays below 3% throughout; the paper attributes this to purified triggered samples themselves suppressing backdoor behavior during DiSP's fine-tuning stage. Across four trigger types (default black patch, Gaussian-noise patch (std=30), four-corner multi-patch, and full-image blended trigger), DiSP reduces ASR from over 90% to at or near 0% in each case. An ablation isolating the saliency-based masking component shows that both 'No Masking' and 'Random Masking' variants leave ASR (w/t) at or above 88%, versus <1% for full DiSP, while all variants preserve clean performance within <2% relative CP -- indicating the saliency-based token scoring, not just masked-input purification per se, is essential to DiSP's effectiveness. A supporting analysis (Figure 2) on three backdoored LLaDA-V models shows increasing mask ratio sharply lowers ASR (w/t) while relative clean performance (measured on MMMU) stays at 89-91.3% even at a 45% mask ratio, motivating the design; one noted anomaly is that in the targeted-refusal setting ASR (w/t) rises again at very high (e.g. 75%) mask ratios, attributed to heavy masking itself inducing abstention (which is scored as an 'attack success' for that target) even without a trigger.
core_contribution: The paper (1) is the first to empirically demonstrate that Multimodal Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) are vulnerable to standard data-poisoning backdoor attacks (originally designed for autoregressive models), and (2) proposes DiSP (Diffusion Self-Purification), a backdoor-removal defense tailored to MDLMs. DiSP exploits a novel finding: selectively masking a small subset of high-saliency visual tokens at inference time suppresses trigger-induced behavior in a backdoored MDLM while leaving clean-input behavior largely intact. The saliency of each visual token is estimated via a Fisher-Jacobian quadratic form (a second-order Taylor approximation of the KL-divergence change caused by masking that token), computed efficiently with a Hutchinson trace/probe estimator. DiSP then (a) runs masked-input inference on the compromised model over its own (poisoned) training set to rewrite triggered responses into their purified, untriggered form, producing a purified dataset, and (b) fine-tunes the compromised model on this purified dataset to remove the backdoor -- without any auxiliary model, external clean data, or knowledge of poisoning rate/trigger pattern/attack target.
threat_model: Data-poisoning backdoor threat model (following prior work, cited as refs [16] and [45] in the paper). The adversary can arbitrarily inject a proportion of poisoned image-text-response triples into an otherwise clean visual-instruction-tuning dataset (e.g., via public dataset releases or fine-tuning-as-a-service platforms), but must follow the standard training pipeline (cannot alter loss/training code). The defender has full control over training and full access to the (possibly poisoned) training dataset, but has no prior knowledge of which samples are poisoned, the poisoning rate, the trigger pattern, or the attack target, and is not given any additional clean reference data. Three backdoor targets are studied: targeted refusal (denial-of-service-style abstention), content insertion (prepending a fixed token sequence, e.g. "<backdoor>"), and semantic misclassification (mapping one image category to another, e.g. dog->boat). Default trigger is a 20x20 black patch; noise-patch, multi-patch, and blended triggers are also tested.
limitations: The paper's stated scope is narrow: two MDLM architectures (LLaDA-V, LaViDa), only projector-level fine-tuning for backdoor implantation (not full-model fine-tuning), and image-patch/blend triggers only (no textual or cross-modal triggers). The threat model assumes the defender has full access to the entire (poisoned) training set and full control of training -- it does not address settings where the defender only has query access to a deployed model or partial data visibility. The paper itself flags an anomaly where DiSP's own masking mechanism induces false 'attack success' at very high mask ratios for the targeted-refusal target, an artifact of using abstention as the attack objective. The Data-filtering/BYE baseline is compared only as an oracle upper bound (perfect poisoned-sample identification) rather than BYE's actual (non-oracle) detection performance, which likely favors DiSP by understating what a realistic BYE-style filter could achieve if adapted. No evaluation against adaptive attackers aware of the saliency-masking defense (i.e., no adaptive-attack robustness analysis) is presented in the main text portion reviewed. The saliency computation depends on a Hutchinson estimator and top-k logit truncation approximation whose sensitivity to hyperparameters (number of probes m, top-k) is not quantified in the main text.

datasets_used: CC-SBU-Align (1,000 sampled examples, used for content-insertion and targeted-refusal tasks; originally used for MiniGPT-4 visual-instruction tuning)
Flickr8k (500 dog images used as the poisoning pool for the semantic-misclassification task)
MMMU (used to measure Clean Performance / benign-task accuracy)
quantitative_results: LLaDA-V, Backdoored (no defense), Targeted Refusal: CP 48.44, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 98.50
LLaDA-V, DiSP, Targeted Refusal: CP 49.44, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 1.00
LLaDA-V, Backdoored, Content Insertion: CP 49.56, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 92.50
LLaDA-V, DiSP, Content Insertion: CP 49.22, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 0.50
LLaDA-V, Backdoored, Misclassification: CP 47.22, ASR w/o 1.50, ASR w/t 94.50
LLaDA-V, DiSP, Misclassification: CP 47.56, ASR w/o 1.00, ASR w/t 0.50
LaViDa, Backdoored, Targeted Refusal: CP 41.56, ASR w/o 2.50, ASR w/t 93.50
LaViDa, DiSP, Targeted Refusal: CP 43.44, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 1.50
LaViDa, Backdoored, Content Insertion: CP 41.89, ASR w/o 2.50, ASR w/t 93.50
LaViDa, DiSP, Content Insertion: CP 43.00, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 0.00
LaViDa, Backdoored, Misclassification: CP 41.33, ASR w/o 2.00, ASR w/t 90.00
LaViDa, DiSP, Misclassification: CP 41.33, ASR w/o 0.00, ASR w/t 1.00

Trigger-type robustness (LLaDA-V content insertion): Default patch ASR w/t 92.50 -> DiSP 0.50; Noise patch (std=30) ASR w/t 98.50 -> DiSP 0.00; Multi-patch (4 corners) ASR w/t 95.00 -> DiSP 0.00; Blended trigger ASR w/t 93.00 -> DiSP 0.50

Ablation (Figure 4): No-Masking and Random-Masking variants leave ASR w/t >= 88% across all three attack targets vs. <1% for full DiSP, with <2% variation in relative clean performance across all variants
Mask-ratio sensitivity (Figure 2): at 45% masking ratio, relative clean performance on MMMU remains 89.0-91.3% across the three attack targets while ASR w/t drops sharply
baselines_compared: Random drop (Zeng et al. [64]) -- drops 20% of training data at random
Pruning (BackdoorLLM benchmark [29]) -- prunes 30% of smallest-magnitude model weights
Data filtering / BYE (Rong et al. [45]) -- state-of-the-art AR-only defense, evaluated here as an oracle upper bound with perfect poisoned-sample identification since BYE cannot run natively on MDLMs
